owasp_top10: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
mitre_attack: T1059.004 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell
cwe: CWE-78 - Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command
CWE-77 - Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in a Command
CWE-88 - Improper Neutralization of Argument Delimiters in a Command

threat.title: OS Command Injection via Unsanitized User Input in Express.js and Flask
threat.description: When web applications pass unsanitized user input directly to system shell commands through Node.js child_process or Python subprocess with shell=True, attackers can inject arbitrary operating system commands. This enables complete server compromise, including data exfiltration, system manipulation, lateral movement, and persistent backdoor installation. Command injection remains one of the most critical web application vulnerabilities with direct RCE (Remote Code Execution) capabilities.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker identifies API endpoint that accepts user input for system operations (file processing, network diagnostics, image conversion)
2. Discovers application uses child_process.exec() or subprocess with shell=True to execute commands
3. Tests for command injection by injecting shell metacharacters (; | & $ ` \n) in input parameters
4. Chains multiple commands using separators: ; && || | to execute arbitrary code
5. Establishes reverse shell connection to attacker-controlled server for persistent access
6. Escalates privileges, exfiltrates sensitive data (database credentials, API keys, customer data), and deploys cryptominers or ransomware
prerequisites: Application executes system commands with user-controllable input
Uses shell interpretation (child_process.exec, subprocess with shell=True)
No allowlist validation for command parameters
Application runs with elevated privileges or has access to sensitive resources

cve_references.description: Node.js command injection via args parameter of child_process.spawn without shell option on Windows. Due to improper handling of batch files, malicious command line arguments can inject arbitrary commands even when shell option is disabled.
Incomplete fix for CVE-2024-27980 (BatBadBut vulnerability). Command injection via improper handling of batch files with all possible extensions on Windows in child_process.spawn/spawnSync.
Apache Airflow command injection vulnerability. Example DAGs did not properly sanitize user-provided params, making them susceptible to OS Command Injection from the web UI.
Apache Struts2 remote code execution via Jakarta Multipart parser. Attackers exploited this by sending malicious Content-Type headers, leading to the Equifax breach.

real_world_breaches.impact: 143 million people affected; $700 million settlement; exploited Apache Struts command injection vulnerability (CVE-2017-5638)
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability allowing remote code execution through malicious Content-Type headers. Patch was available March 7, attack began March 10.

real_world_breaches.impact: Over 100 million customers affected; $190 million settlement; involved SSRF leading to metadata access and potential command injection
real_world_breaches.root_cause: SSRF vulnerability combined with misconfigured WAF allowed attacker to access AWS metadata service and execute unauthorized commands, leading to S3 bucket access

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Command injection in file processing endpoint allowing arbitrary code execution via filename parameter

security_research.title: Command Injection in 2024: Vulnerability Analysis Report
security_research.key_finding: 7% of all vulnerabilities in open-source projects in 2024 were command injection, increasing from 2,348 (2023) to 2,600 (2024)

security_research.title: Node.js Security: Command Injection Vulnerabilities
security_research.key_finding: Child_process.spawn and spawnSync remain vulnerable to command injection on Windows even without shell option enabled

mitigations.title: Use Safe APIs with Parameterized Commands (Array Arguments)
mitigations.description: Replace shell-based command execution with safe APIs that accept arguments as arrays. Node.js child_process.spawn() and Python subprocess.run() with shell=False prevent shell interpretation and command injection by treating each array element as a literal argument.
nist_csf: PR.IP-1 - A baseline configuration is created and maintained
PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
validation_steps: Replace all child_process.exec() calls with spawn()
Ensure spawn() is called without {shell: true} option
Pass command arguments as array, not concatenated strings
Add input validation before executing commands
Test with injection payloads: ; cat /etc/passwd | whoami

mitigations.title: Implement Strict Input Validation with Allowlists
mitigations.description: Validate all user input against strict allowlists (not denylists) before passing to system commands. Use regex patterns, enum values, or predefined options to ensure only expected values are processed. Never rely on blocklists of dangerous characters, as they can be bypassed.
validation_steps: Define allowlists for all command parameters
Implement regex validation for filenames and paths

mitigations.title: Deploy Sandboxed Execution Environments
mitigations.description: Execute system commands in isolated sandboxed environments with restricted permissions, resource limits, and no network access. Use Docker containers, seccomp profiles, or dedicated sandboxing libraries to limit blast radius of successful command injection.

indicators: Unusual child process spawning patterns (sh, bash, cmd.exe from web application)
Network connections initiated by spawned processes
File access patterns outside application directory
Multiple failed command executions with special characters
Processes with unusual arguments containing shell metacharacters
Execution of common post-exploitation tools (nc, curl, wget, powershell)
Reverse shell connections to external IPs

log_queries.query:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4688
| where ParentProcessName contains "node" or ParentProcessName contains "python"
| where ProcessCommandLine contains_any (";", "|", "&&", "$(", "`")
| where ProcessCommandLine contains_any ("bash", "sh", "cmd", "nc", "wget", "curl")
| extend SuspicionScore = case(
    ProcessCommandLine contains "nc", 10,
    ProcessCommandLine contains "/etc/passwd", 10,
    ProcessCommandLine contains "wget", 8,
    ProcessCommandLine contains "bash -i", 9,
    5)
| where SuspicionScore >= 7
| summarize Count=count() by Computer, Account, ProcessCommandLine, SuspicionScore
| order by SuspicionScore desc
process.parent.name:("node" OR "python") AND process.name:("sh" OR "bash" OR "nc") AND process.command_line:(*;* OR *|* OR *&&*)

index=linux sourcetype=linux_audit type=EXECVE
| where (ppid_name="node" OR ppid_name="python")
  AND (command="sh" OR command="bash" OR command="nc")
  AND (arg1 LIKE "%;&" OR arg1 LIKE "%|%")
| stats count by host, user, command

tools.name: osquery
tools.usage: SELECT * FROM processes WHERE parent IN (SELECT pid FROM processes WHERE name='node')
